perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to maintain thirty (30) days of stock in Distributor's inventory to support sales.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will endeavor to maintain some stock in Distributor's inventory to support sales.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses the term "commercially reasonable efforts to maintain thirty (30) days of stock". The change replaces this with "endeavor to maintain some stock", making the obligation much weaker and undefined. "Some stock" is not defined, and "endeavor" does not create a clear obligation to actually maintain any specific level of stock.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly stated, a requirement to keep thirty days of stock could be argued as part of the implied duty of good faith. Removing the obligation to maintain a reasonable stock level, and replacing it with simply trying to maintain 'some stock' undermines the good faith effort of fulfilling the agreement. If the Distributor held no stock, the Zebra might argue that they were in breach of contract. Under the UCC, all contracts imply a duty of good faith, which is undermined by allowing the distributor to effectively avoid stocking the product.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation on the distributor, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith under UCC. The original text required "commercially reasonable efforts to maintain thirty (30) days of stock", while the changed text only requires the distributor to "endeavor to maintain some stock." This introduces ambiguity and a weaker commitment. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." By reducing the stock level obligation to an undefined "some stock" and merely endeavoring to maintain it, the distributor could potentially avoid stocking any meaningful amount of product, undermining the good faith performance expected under the UCC, and creating an Ambiguous Legal Obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Xplore shall provide to Distributor, at no charge, a means for confirming proof of delivery for Xplore Product shipments when requested by Distributor.
perturbation.changed_text: Xplore will attempt to provide Distributor, at no charge, with a means for confirming proof of delivery for Xplore Product shipments when requested by Distributor.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall provide" to "will attempt to provide" introduces ambiguity.  It weakens Xplore's obligation to actually provide proof of delivery.  It could be interpreted as Xplore only needing to make an effort, but not necessarily succeeding in providing the proof of delivery.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Failure to Perform Material Term
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the UCC, a party has the right to adequate assurance of performance. If proof of delivery is critical for the distributor (ScanSource) to ensure that they are paid by their customers, the weakened obligation means that they might not be able to get assurance and thus possibly halt sales. The UCC requires the other party to assure and deliver the goods. By not being required to provide proof of delivery, it becomes difficult to hold Xplore liable if the shipment does not reach the correct party, or at all.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-609. Right to Adequate Assurance of Performance. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-609. Right to Adequate Assurance of Performance. (1) A contract for sale imposes an obligation on each party that the other's expectation of receiving due performance will not be impaired. When reasonable grounds for insecurity arise with respect to the performance of either party the other may in writing demand adequate assurance of due performance and until he receives such assurance may if commercially reasonable suspend any performance for which he has not already received the agreed return. (2) Between merchants the reasonableness of grounds for insecurity and the adequacy of any assurance offered shall be determined according to commercial standards. (3)Acceptance of any improper delivery or payment does not prejudice the aggrieved party's right to demand adequate assurance of future performance. (4) After receipt of a justified demand failure to provide within a reasonable time not exceeding thirty days such assurance of due performance as is adequate under the circumstances of the particular case is a repudiation of the contract . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall provide" to "will attempt to provide" weakens Xplore's obligation, introducing ambiguity about whether they are actually required to deliver proof of shipment. The scraped snippet from UCC § 2-609(1) states that a contract for sale imposes an obligation on each party that the other's expectation of receiving due performance will not be impaired. By weakening the obligation to provide proof of delivery, the modified text impairs the distributor's expectation of receiving due performance, which is a contradiction of the law.

perturbation.original_text: Orders received without this information or which contain any discrepancy may be returned to Distributor for completion or revision as applicable.
perturbation.changed_text: Orders received without this information or which contain any discrepancy can be handled at Xplore's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that incorrect orders 'may be returned to Distributor for completion'. The changed text says that they 'can be handled at Xplore's discretion'. This removes the requirement to return them and gives Xplore complete discretion on how to handle incorrect orders. This is an issue, because Xplore could claim to not receive the order, or delay it, which breaks the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing Xplore complete discretion on handling incorrect orders, Xplore can effectively sabotage the ability of the distributor to submit orders. The implied duty of good faith under the UCC requires both parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of the contract. Allowing Xplore to potentially ignore or delay orders without any obligation to return them for correction violates this duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by granting Xplore complete discretion in handling incorrect orders, contradicts the implied duty of good faith under the UCC, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The modification allows Xplore to potentially ignore or delay orders without obligation, violating this duty, and thus creating a contradiction.
